does: This paper proposes Wasserstein distributionally robust regret optimization (DRRO) as a framework for mitigating reward overoptimization in reinforcement learning from human feedback. Rather than pessimizing worst-case value as in standard distributionally robust optimization, the method pessimizes worst-case regret relative to the best policy under plausible reward perturbations within a Wasserstein ball. The authors formulate the problem through a simplex allocation model that operates at the prompt level, developing a new optimization criterion that balances robustness against reward misspecification while avoiding excessive conservatism that plagues existing uncertainty-penalty and pessimistic-reward approaches.
connects: This work addresses the fundamental problem of objective misspecification in RLHF, extending the operations research literature on distributionally robust optimization to the reinforcement learning setting. It builds on recent work identifying Goodharting and reward overoptimization as critical failure modes in policy learning from learned proxies, and responds to limitations of prior mitigation strategies including uncertainty penalties, pessimistic rewards, and conservative policy constraints. The paper brings tools from decision-making under uncertainty and robust optimization into dialogue with contemporary challenges in large language model alignment, combining the regret-optimization perspective from online learning with the distributional robustness framework to create a more computationally tractable alternative to worst-case value optimization.
enables: This approach enables more practical and less overly-conservative training procedures for aligned language models by reducing computational burden while maintaining robustness guarantees. The regret-relative perspective could inspire follow-up work developing tractable algorithms for solving the DRRO problem at scale and investigating how regret-robust objectives extend to other learning-from-feedback settings beyond RLHF. Future research might explore adaptive Wasserstein ball radii, incorporation of uncertainty structure about specific types of reward error, and empirical validation on large language models to quantify improvements in Goodhart resistance without sacrificing performance on true objectives. The framework also opens questions about optimal robustness-performance tradeoffs and whether regret-based robustness extends naturally to multi-objective alignment problems.
